summaries.headline: Learning Drives Global AI Adoption in Education
summaries.summary: Google's third annual global survey of 21,000 participants across 21 countries shows learning has overtaken entertainment as the primary reason people use AI tools. Students, teachers, and parents report higher AI usage rates than the general public—85 percent of students, 81 percent of teachers, and 76 percent of parents—with most saying AI improves rather than harms learning, while a Northern Ireland pilot found teachers saved an average of 10 hours per week using AI tools. The shift from curiosity-driven experimentation to purposeful learning suggests AI adoption in education has moved beyond early adopters to become normalized practice across diverse global markets.

summaries.headline: Language Platform Raises $150M Backing Human-AI Teaching Model
summaries.summary: Preply secured $150 million in Series D funding, reaching a $1.2 billion valuation as investors back education models that combine human instruction with AI support rather than full automation. The online language learning platform connects students with tutors across more than 90 languages in 180 countries, using AI for lesson planning and progress tracking while keeping human teachers central to instruction. The funding reflects growing investor confidence in "human-led, AI-augmented" approaches as districts and families seek personalized learning that maintains the relationship and cultural components that automated systems cannot replicate.

summaries.headline: Delaware Proposes $400M Boost for High-Needs Students
summaries.summary: Delaware's Public Education Funding Commission is developing a hybrid formula that would direct $200 million to $400 million in additional funding toward low-income and multilingual learners, moving away from the state's 1940s-era unit-based system. Commission leaders point to neighboring states like Maryland, New Jersey, and Pennsylvania, where higher per-pupil spending targeted at high-needs students correlates with better test scores, graduation rates, and post-secondary outcomes. The proposal would give districts more flexibility in spending while maintaining the foundation of the original formula, potentially positioning Delaware to close performance gaps with regional competitors.

summaries.headline: Oregon Lawmakers Target 27-Year-Old School Funding Formula
summaries.summary: Oregon legislators are proposing to eliminate the state's Quality Education Model and Quality Education Commission, which have guided school funding decisions since 1999, replacing them with contracted research conducted every eight years. The current system has only been fully funded once in its 27-year history, when lawmakers approved a record $11.4 billion for schools in 2025. The proposed overhaul would shift funding research from the Oregon Department of Education to the Legislative Policy and Research Office, giving lawmakers just one month to debate changes that would fundamentally reshape how the state determines what schools need to operate effectively.

summaries.headline: Visual Literacy Emerges as Essential AI-Era Reading Skill
summaries.summary: Educators are positioning visual literacy as a core reading strategy to help students navigate an increasingly AI-saturated information environment where realistic-looking but potentially fabricated images appear across learning materials and social media. Students who struggle with traditional text-based reading tasks often engage more readily when learning to "read" images by slowing down, describing what they see, making inferences, and justifying their thinking—the same cognitive strategies required for written comprehension. The shift reflects growing recognition that critical evaluation of visual content has become as fundamental as traditional literacy skills in preparing students to identify misinformation and question constructed media.

summaries.headline: Financial Literacy Requirements Expand Across Most States
summaries.summary: Thirty-one states now require high school students to take a financial literacy course for graduation, with Hawaii becoming the latest to add the mandate starting in the 2026-2027 school year. Only Alaska, Wyoming, and Washington D.C. currently have no financial literacy requirements, while other states require courses to be offered or standards to be implemented at the district level. The expansion comes as national financial literacy rates remain stagnant at 49 percent since 2017, suggesting state mandates may be responding to persistent gaps in students' money management skills.

summaries.headline: New Hampshire Voucher Growth Signals Christian School Expansion
summaries.summary: Laconia Christian Academy in New Hampshire reported 130 percent enrollment growth since 2020, with nearly all students using state Education Freedom Account vouchers that provide taxpayer funding regardless of family income. New Hampshire became the 18th state with universal private school choice in June, joining a post-pandemic expansion of voucher programs that now includes President Trump's planned federal voucher initiative starting in 2027. The growth pattern at schools like Laconia suggests these universal programs may reshape the private education landscape, particularly benefiting religious institutions that can now access broader student populations through public funding.

summaries.headline: New Haven Schools Build Trust Amid Immigration Enforcement
summaries.summary: New Haven public schools have developed comprehensive support systems for immigrant families, including know-your-rights sessions, legal resource cards, and direct advocacy when students or family members face detention. English language learner enrollment dropped 7.3 percent in New Haven and nearly 3.8 percent statewide between fall 2024 and fall 2025, with many expected families simply disappearing from schools. The district's deep community relationships and immediate response protocols offer a model for how schools can maintain educational access while navigating federal immigration enforcement.

summaries.headline: Dothan Board Rejects New Superintendent's Math Literacy Plan
summaries.summary: The Dothan City School Board blocked major portions of Superintendent Dr. Garrick Askew's 100-day improvement plan, specifically rejecting proposals to hire consultants for math and literacy interventions in a district with struggling academic performance. Board members cited teacher opposition to consultants based on past unsuccessful experiences and federal funding restrictions that prevented using Title 2 money for the proposed contracts, though they approved hiring incentives and an assistant superintendent position. The rejection highlights the tension between new leadership's turnaround strategies and veteran board members' preference for internal solutions, particularly in a district that has cycled through five superintendents in 11 years.

summaries.headline: Smartgrade Partners with CUSP on Curriculum Assessments
summaries.summary: Assessment platform Smartgrade has partnered with CUSP, a curriculum organization working with more than 700 UK schools, to integrate curriculum-aligned assessments with data analysis tools. The collaboration allows schools using CUSP's evidence-based curriculum frameworks to upload assessment data through Smartgrade's platform and compare results with other CUSP schools to identify curriculum gaps. The partnership addresses a persistent challenge in education by connecting what schools teach with how they measure student learning, potentially offering clearer insights into curriculum effectiveness at both classroom and system levels.

summaries.headline: Missouri Tech Leaders Meet on Student Data Protection
summaries.summary: School technology leaders gathered in Neosho to address strengthening student data protection protocols across their districts. The meeting focused on developing comprehensive strategies to safeguard sensitive student information as schools increasingly rely on digital platforms and data management systems. The regional collaboration signals growing recognition that data security requires coordinated effort beyond individual district boundaries, particularly as cyber threats targeting educational institutions continue to escalate.

summaries.headline: Google Adds AI Detection, Security Tools for Schools
summaries.summary: Google rolled out new security and AI detection features for its Workspace for Education platform this week, including SynthID technology that can identify images and videos created or edited by Google AI. The updates also include ransomware detection for Google Drive, consolidated security data through SecOps connector for Education Plus and Standard users, and enhanced access controls for Google Meet that let hosts limit participation by user groups rather than entire domains. The moves signal Google's response to growing district concerns about both AI-generated content in classrooms and cybersecurity threats targeting school systems.

summaries.headline: New SEL Provider Launches K-12 Embedded Services Model
summaries.summary: Transformative Growth announced a formal K-12 partnership model that embeds mental health professionals and social-emotional learning programming directly within school settings. The framework allows districts to implement services at individual schools or district-wide, emphasizing equity by prioritizing partnerships with districts serving diverse and underserved populations. The model positions SEL and mental health support as integrated rather than separate services, reflecting districts' need for comprehensive solutions that minimize instructional disruption while maximizing student access to care.

summaries.headline: Two D.C. Schools Cut Chronic Absenteeism in Half
summaries.summary: Center City Congress Heights and D.C. Prep Anacostia have achieved the lowest chronic absenteeism rates of any schools east of the Anacostia River, despite serving high-poverty Ward 8 communities where half of students are chronically absent. Both schools saw their chronic absenteeism rates double after the pandemic but have since driven them down through deliberate, sustained strategies built on the belief that attendance is everyone's responsibility and that trust-building must precede any intervention. The progress stands out in a city where chronic absenteeism still hovers near 40 percent and comes as research shows each missed day of class costs students one point on D.C.'s standardized assessment.

summaries.headline: Community Volunteers Fill Adult English Learning Gaps
summaries.summary: Literacy Volunteers of Charlottesville/Albemarle supports hundreds of adults annually with English skills and citizenship preparation, but maintains a waiting list due to high demand for services. The organization requires volunteers to commit two hours weekly with students, providing training and materials to tutors who help community members develop language skills for family and civic participation. The persistent waiting list signals both strong community need for adult English learning support and the capacity constraints facing local organizations that serve immigrant and adult learner populations.

summaries.headline: Oaklyn District Adds Literacy Tools, Reports Attendance Gains
summaries.summary: Oaklyn school leaders introduced three new early childhood programs this year to meet state literacy requirements and address social-emotional learning needs, including Kickstart Literacy for preschool and GOLDfinch assessment tools for tracking letter and sound knowledge. The district reported fewer kindergarten behavior issues and improvements in chronic absenteeism, while expanding their Creative Curriculum model from preschool into kindergarten with project-based learning approaches. The coordinated focus on both academic foundations and social-emotional skills reflects how small districts are adapting comprehensive early learning strategies to address multiple student needs simultaneously.

summaries.headline: Former Flipkart CTO Launches AI STEM Tutor
summaries.summary: Former Flipkart CTO Peeyush Ranjan has launched Fermi.ai, an AI tutoring platform focused on high school mathematics, physics, and chemistry that guides students to solutions rather than providing direct answers. A three-month pilot with 79 students showed students initially scoring 2 out of 10 improved to an average of 6.7, while those starting at 5 out of 10 reached nearly 8 out of 10. The platform launches as free trials in the US and India at a time when schools face pressure to integrate AI tools that students are already adopting independently.

summaries.headline: Tanzania EdTech Fellowship Opens Third Cohort Applications
summaries.summary: The Mastercard Foundation opened applications for its third Tanzania EdTech Fellowship cohort, targeting growth-stage education technology companies already demonstrating measurable impact in the region. The program provides up to $70,000 in equity-free funding plus technical support, business mentorship, and regulatory guidance to help companies reach 8,000-10,000 learners and achieve commercial sustainability. Applications close January 31, continuing a multi-year effort to scale locally founded solutions that address learning access and outcomes for underserved populations across East Africa.

summaries.headline: AI-Native Networks Cut Manual IT Tasks for Districts
summaries.summary: District IT teams are deploying AI-informed networking platforms to automate troubleshooting and reduce the manual maintenance that strains thin-stretched technology staff. At Prosper Independent School District in Texas, leaders replaced failing controller architecture with Juniper Networks' Mist AI after experiencing daily wireless outages across multiple campuses serving 32,000 users. The AI system pinpoints network problems in three clicks, drilling down from district-wide views to individual classroom access points and resolving issues with minimal human intervention.

summaries.headline: Oregon Lawmakers Target Decades-Old School Funding Formula
summaries.summary: Oregon lawmakers are considering legislation that would replace the state's Quality Education Model, which has guided education funding discussions since 1999, with a system more directly tied to student outcomes and long-term accountability. Rep. Ricki Ruiz, a sponsor of the proposal, argues the current model has produced repeated debates over "adequate funding" without delivering consistent results for students. The shift would move Oregon away from input-based funding toward performance-driven allocation, potentially reshaping how districts approach resource allocation and educational priorities.

synthesis.topic_title: School Funding & Budgets: States Target Mandate Relief and Local Control
synthesis.whats_happening: State legislators are launching early-session efforts to address school funding adequacy while simultaneously targeting outdated educational mandates that burden districts. Local officials are stepping into more visible roles communicating funding decisions directly to their communities.
synthesis.whats_driving: Post-pandemic budget pressures have exposed structural funding inadequacies in public education systems, particularly in states with historically underfunded schools. The combination of inflation, enrollment shifts, and compliance costs is pushing lawmakers to reconsider both funding formulas and regulatory frameworks that constrain local flexibility.

[Facebook](https://aldailynews.com/#facebook "Facebook") [Twitter](https://aldailynews.com/#twitter "Twitter") [Mastodon](https://aldailynews.com/#mastodon "Mastodon") [Email](https://aldailynews.com/#email "Email") [Share](https://www.addtoany.com/share#url=https%3A%2F%2Faldailynews.com%2Falabama-house-education-budget-chairman-targets-outdated-mandates-local-control-in-early-session%2F&title=Alabama%20House%20education%20budget%20chairman%20targets%20outdated%20mandates%2C%20local%20control%20early%20in%20session)

BIRMINGHAM, Ala. – Alabama House education budget committee Chairman Danny Garrett, R-Trussville, is pushing for passage of bills he has filed to give schools more autonomy and flexibility while adding more transparency for the public.

Taken together, the measures reflect Garrett’s broader push to reduce statutory mandates in education law and shift more decision-making authority to local school systems and higher education institutions. As education budget chairman, he said that flexibility is increasingly important as schools face shifting enrollment and uncertainty around funding.

All four bills are expected in House committees this week.

[House Bill 152](https://alison.legislature.state.al.us/files/pdf/SearchableInstruments/2026RS/HB152-int.pdf) would undo a 1965 law mandating the University of Alabama to have a standalone graduate school of social work. Garrett said the mandate was created at a time when the state needed more social workers, but that he believes the requirement is no longer necessary.

“They’re a little bit handcuffed by the fact that we require them to have that on the books,” Garrett said. If the bill becomes law, UA would have the option to continue operating a standalone School of Social Work or to house the program within a different college.

UA officials said they look forward to continuing to graduate “highly skilled, highly versatile social work practitioners.”

“We will continue to build upon that foundation, and this statutory change will provide the University autonomy over curriculum, organization and continued innovation in meeting the needs of the state and our students,” officials said in a statement to ADN.

A second bill, [House Bill 151](https://alison.legislature.state.al.us/files/pdf/SearchableInstruments/2026RS/HB151-int.pdf), removes the mandate for the University of Alabama to maintain a Physicians Advisory Board, first required by law in 1943. If UA wants to keep the board, they can do so, Garrett said, but it will be up to them to decide.

“This just says it’s your business, not ours,” he said.

[House Bill 178](https://alison.legislature.state.al.us/files/pdf/SearchableInstruments/2026RS/HB178-int.pdf) would allow city school systems to consolidate if they choose to do so.

“Under current law, a county and city system can combine,” he said.

Garrett said it is likely time that some smaller city school systems consider combining resources to better serve students.

“I want to give cities the opportunity,” he said. “I want the public education people to (see that they have) all the tools in the toolbox.”

For now, Education Trust Fund revenues are “holding their own” and growing a little, he said, but that could change. He believes those revenues are divided over too many systems.

“They need to stretch the dollars they get further,” he said. “So it’s possible that there may be two cities that realize together they might have more money to put into the classroom and more money for what they need.”

According to an Alabama Daily News analysis, 13 school districts – six cities and seven counties – enroll fewer than 1,000 students. Four of those have fewer than 750 students:

- Linden City schools in Marengo County: 402 students in three schools,
- Barbour County schools: 550 students in two schools,
- Elba City schools in Coffee County: 588 students in two schools, and
- Perry County schools: 740 students in two schools

As part of the broader effort to inform the public, Garrett said he wants Alabamians to understand how dependent the state is on federal funding.

He filed [House Bill 165](https://alison.legislature.state.al.us/files/pdf/SearchableInstruments/2026RS/HB165-int.pdf) to require postsecondary education institutions to break down and report how much federal funding they receive, how it is used and how they would respond if that funding were reduced.

“I think a lot of people are not aware of how heavily dependent our state is on federal dollars,” he said.

Senate Education Budget Chair Arthur Orr, R-Decatur, is sponsoring the same bill in the upper chamber.

One piece of legislation that has not yet been filed, but will be “very soon,” Garrett said, is a bill that would incentivize colleges and universities to improve outcomes for graduates through a performance-based funding system.

A joint committee of education budget committee members met twice in recent months to hear from experts about how to structure that type of funding. In 2024, performance-based funding was distributed to two-year colleges in 28 states and in four-year colleges in 21 states, according to the State Higher Education Executive Officers Association.

Beyond passing these bills, Garrett said his priorities for this session are making sure the CHOOSE Act – which created education savings accounts – and the RAISE Act – which provides additional funding to K-12 public schools for high-needs students – are properly funded. He said he is also looking back at previous investments the legislature and taxpayers have made in the state’s K-12 through postsecondary systems.

_Updated 11:55 a.m. with statement from University of Alabama officials._

LENOIR CITY, Tenn. — Lenoir City Mayor Tony Aikens has shared the following statement regarding city school funding.

### CONSERVATIVE APPROACH

"I am emphasizing a 'wait-and-see' approach to fiscal responsibility — specifically regarding the timing of school funding and bond issuance," the mayor shared with The News-Herald in a Jan. 15 release.

Based on the statements that have been shared on social media, Aikens provided the following breakdown of the key points that he said he and several City Council members are using to justify their decision to delay the project:

**HISTORICAL PRECEDENT:** "I dispute the claim this is the first time that Council has pushed back on school funding, citing a previous instance where the school system was asked to tighten its budget before approval."

**FINANCIAL SAVINGS:** "By waiting until now, the city has saved over $2 million."

**INTEREST RATES:** "I believe that interest rates will continue to trend downward. My logic is that waiting a few more months could result in lower borrowing costs, potentially saving millions on long-term debt service."

**THE 'LOUDON COUNTY FACTOR':** "The Loudon County Commission recently increased your taxes to build a new county high school that is not needed. This is a reason to be extra cautious with Lenoir City taxpayers' money."

**PHASED CONSTRUCTION:** "I suggest that the school board should consider breaking the project into phases rather than doing it all at once to manage costs better."

### WHAT THIS MEANS TO TAXPAYERS

"The central tension here is between immediate infrastructure needs and market timing," Mayor Aikens stated. "The City Council and I are trying to be good stewards for your taxpayer dollars; shouldn't a true, professional needs assessment be completed before a decision is made?"

### IN CLOSING

"I have already reached out to the director of schools and chairman of the board to let them know that I welcome an open dialogue on these concerns."

The submitted statement was signed "Sincerely, Mayor Tony R Aikens."

_EDITOR'S NOTE: This online article will be printed fully in the Jan. 28, 2026, edition of the Loudon County News-Herald._

West Virginia Board of Education President Paul Hardesty was sounding the alarm on public school funding back in early October. Many others have issued similar warnings for years.

Fast forward to this week, when one of the first actions of the fledgling 2026 West Virginia legislative session came via emergency procedure in the House of Delegates, as lawmakers passed bills to provide $8 million to the Hancock County School District so it could make payroll in February.

It's hard to recall a time when a school district, not a private business, was so hard up financially that employees had to worry about whether their paychecks would clear or if they'd be issued at all.

In this case, officials say the problem is not one of funding, but financial mismanagement. The West Virginia Board of Education took over operations of Hancock County Schools last week and fired the superintendent.

West Virginia Watch reporter Amelia Ferrell Knisely [noted](https://westvirginiawatch.com/2026/01/19/wv-house-swiftly-approves-8m-in-funding-to-help-hancock-county-pay-teachers-amid-financial-issues/) state officials said the district had 143 more employees than it should've under the school aid funding formula and had been using federal relief dollars from the COVID-19 pandemic to cover costs, but is now facing a $5 million cash shortfall. Knisely reported the school district also has ongoing construction project costs than must be covered.

Mismanaged finances aside, just about every public school district in West Virginia is facing hard times economically. Kanawha County Schools recently went through a round of consolidations to lower costs, and the state Board of Education approved more than 10 consolidation plans in six counties last month.

The problem is evident. West Virginia's population continues to decline — one of only three states to do so in the latest census — and at a rate higher than any other state in the U.S. This results in fewer children attending public schools. Enrollment in also dropping because of options like public charter schools, private schools and homeschooling. Those alternatives have become even more appealing in recent years because of the Hope Scholarship, a voucher program that directs public money to students to help cover the cost of private schools or homeschooling.

Dropping public school enrollment means less money for those schools, because funding is directly tied to the number of students in a given district.

Hardesty made the argument that the situation is even more unfair because public schools, as they should be, are governed by a volume of statutes and regulations under state law, while charter schools and homeschools are not.

At that meeting, [in a clip](https://wchstv.com/newsletter-daily/state-boe-president-calls-on-legislature-to-level-the-playing-field-for-public-education) that is now circulating around social media again because the legislative session is underway, Hardesty placed a thick, hardbound copy of the school code book on his desk, then stacked five years' worth of "green books," — volumes that contain new laws that affect public education in a given legislative session — on top of that. Those laws cover everything from hiring practices to building codes to curriculum. He then produced the laws and regulations that cover public charter schools, which fit on a page-and-a-half of the front and back of a single, regular sheet of paper. After that, he pulled out the laws governing home schools, which fit on an index card.

Hardesty remarked he doesn't have a problem with school choice, "but the choices have to be fair and equitable."

His visual demonstration drove home how unfair the situation has become.

Hardesty added he and his department would be a willing parter with the Legislature in finding a solution, but that solution must involve addressing the school aid formula and, if it didn't, "don't waste my time and don't waste your time."

The question then becomes how to address the school aid formula, and lawmakers aren't exactly rushing to file bills in that vein. In fact, as Hardesty himself recently pointed out, the last time the school aid formula was changed was in 1982.

This is a big problem that will take time to fix. But those first steps need to be taken soon. West Virginia consistently ranks near the bottom of the country in education, a key metric linked to quality of life and a child's future success. Schools are understaffed. Teachers are underpaid and perform tasks that go well beyond their traditional job description. It's been this way for years. West Virginia has to start digging itself out of this hole, because when public schools vanish, kids and communities suffer. They're already suffering. It can't continue this way.
